_description: Design tokens for the disabled-state-contrast pattern. Disabled element colors are used in after/styles.css and represent best-practice readable values even though WCAG 1.4.3 exempts natively disabled controls.

text.usage: Text color for natively disabled inputs and buttons
text.note: On #f3f4f6 = 3.39:1. WCAG 1.4.3 exempts natively disabled controls — no contrast requirement applies. If you use aria-disabled without the native disabled attribute, you must meet 4.5:1; use #767676 (4.54:1 on white) instead. No wcag field because the exemption makes a WCAG level claim misleading.

background.usage: Background for disabled fields and buttons
background.note: Subtle gray tint distinguishes disabled from active fields.

border.note: Same as active field border — removed visual 'weight' comes from the muted text color, not a lighter border.

fail-example-text.usage: Shown in before/ — CSS-only disabled state without the disabled attribute
fail-example-text.note: Classic 'silver gray' used for disabled states. On white = 1.82:1 (FAIL). Invisible to low-vision users. When the native disabled attribute is absent, axe checks and flags this.

hint.note: 4.84:1 on white — passes AA. Verify separately if used on tinted backgrounds.

primary-text.usage: White text on primary button (#1d4ed8 background)
primary-text.note: White on #1d4ed8 = 6.70:1 (AA). No wcag field — validator checks against white (1:1); this token is only meaningful against its button background.
